title: Wireless Deployment
description: Test your knowledge of wireless network deployment including site surveys, channel planning, access point placement, heat maps, and regulatory features like DFS and TPC.
starterCode:
# WIRELESS DEPLOYMENT EXERCISE

## Part 1: Site Surveys

1. A _______ site survey involves physically walking through the facility with survey equipment.

2. A _______ site survey uses software modeling without visiting the location.

3. An active site survey measures actual _______ by connecting to access points.

4. A passive site survey listens for _______ without connecting to any AP.

5. Site survey tools generate visual _______ maps showing signal coverage areas.

## Part 2: Channel Planning (2.4 GHz)

6. The 2.4 GHz band has _______ total channels available (in North America).

7. The three non-overlapping channels in 2.4 GHz are 1, _______, and 11.

8. Each 2.4 GHz channel has a width of _______ MHz.

9. Adjacent APs should use non-overlapping channels to prevent co-channel _______.

10. The recommended overlap between AP coverage areas is _______% to 20%.

## Part 3: Channel Planning (5 GHz and 6 GHz)

11. The 5 GHz band offers significantly more _______ channels than 2.4 GHz.

12. 5 GHz channels can operate at widths of 20, 40, 80, or _______ MHz.

13. DFS stands for Dynamic _______ Selection.

14. DFS requires APs to vacate channels when _______ signals are detected.

15. TPC stands for Transmit _______ Control.

16. Wi-Fi 6E extends into the _______ GHz band for additional spectrum.

## Part 4: Access Point Placement

17. APs should be mounted on _______ rather than under desks for optimal coverage.

18. The recommended AP height for most deployments is _______ to 15 feet.

19. High-density environments like auditoriums require _______ AP power settings.

20. _______ antennas radiate signal in all directions equally on the horizontal plane.

21. _______ antennas focus the signal in a specific direction for longer range.

## Part 5: Heat Maps and Validation

22. A heat map uses color coding where _______ typically represents strong signal.

23. Signal strength is measured in _______ (decibels relative to milliwatts).

24. A good indoor signal strength is typically _______ dBm or stronger (closer to 0).

25. Post-deployment _______ surveys confirm the network meets design specifications.

26. A wireless _______ controller manages multiple APs centrally in enterprise deployments.

## Part 6: Scenario - Campus Wireless Deployment

A university is deploying wireless across 15 buildings to support 30,000 students. The network team must design a scalable, high-density wireless infrastructure.

27. The university selects a _______-based architecture where all APs are managed by a central wireless LAN controller rather than configuring each AP individually.

28. For lecture halls with 300 students, the design calls for one AP per _______ users to ensure adequate bandwidth and airtime fairness.

29. In the 2.4 GHz band, the channel plan assigns only channels 1, _______, and 11 to avoid co-channel interference between adjacent APs.

30. Each AP requires _______ (Power over Ethernet) from the network switch, eliminating the need for separate power outlets at each mounting location.

31. Before deployment, the team conducts a _______ survey using specialized software to create heat maps and validate AP placement.

## Part 7: Scenario - Wireless Roaming Design

The university's network team must ensure seamless wireless connectivity as students move between buildings and floors.

32. The team enables 802.11r (Fast _______ Transition) to reduce roaming handoff time by pre-authenticating with target APs.

33. 802.11k provides Radio _______ Management, allowing APs to send neighbor reports so clients know which APs are available for roaming.

34. 802.11v enables BSS _______ Management, allowing the network to steer clients to less congested APs.

35. To speed up roaming for WPA2-Enterprise, the network caches the PMK using _______ Key Caching (OKC) so clients skip full re-authentication.

36. The roaming _______ is configured to determine the minimum signal strength at which a client should begin looking for a better AP.
solution:
# SOLUTION: WIRELESS DEPLOYMENT

## Part 1: Site Surveys

1. A **active** site survey involves physically walking through the facility with survey equipment. ✓

2. A **predictive** site survey uses software modeling without visiting the location. ✓

3. An active site survey measures actual **throughput** by connecting to access points. ✓

4. A passive site survey listens for **beacons** without connecting to any AP. ✓

5. Site survey tools generate visual **heat** maps showing signal coverage areas. ✓

## Part 2: Channel Planning (2.4 GHz)

6. The 2.4 GHz band has **11** total channels available (in North America). ✓

7. The three non-overlapping channels in 2.4 GHz are 1, **6**, and 11. ✓

8. Each 2.4 GHz channel has a width of **22** MHz. ✓

9. Adjacent APs should use non-overlapping channels to prevent co-channel **interference**. ✓

10. The recommended overlap between AP coverage areas is **10**% to 20%. ✓

## Part 3: Channel Planning (5 GHz and 6 GHz)

11. The 5 GHz band offers significantly more **non-overlapping** channels than 2.4 GHz. ✓

12. 5 GHz channels can operate at widths of 20, 40, 80, or **160** MHz. ✓

13. DFS stands for Dynamic **Frequency** Selection. ✓

14. DFS requires APs to vacate channels when **radar** signals are detected. ✓

15. TPC stands for Transmit **Power** Control. ✓

16. Wi-Fi 6E extends into the **6** GHz band for additional spectrum. ✓

## Part 4: Access Point Placement

17. APs should be mounted on **ceilings** rather than under desks for optimal coverage. ✓

18. The recommended AP height for most deployments is **10** to 15 feet. ✓

19. High-density environments like auditoriums require **lower** AP power settings. ✓

20. **Omnidirectional** antennas radiate signal in all directions equally on the horizontal plane. ✓

21. **Directional** antennas focus the signal in a specific direction for longer range. ✓

## Part 5: Heat Maps and Validation

22. A heat map uses color coding where **green** typically represents strong signal. ✓

23. Signal strength is measured in **dBm** (decibels relative to milliwatts). ✓

24. A good indoor signal strength is typically **-65** dBm or stronger (closer to 0). ✓

25. Post-deployment **validation** surveys confirm the network meets design specifications. ✓

26. A wireless **LAN** controller manages multiple APs centrally in enterprise deployments. ✓

## Part 6: Scenario - Campus Wireless Deployment

A university is deploying wireless across 15 buildings to support 30,000 students. The network team must design a scalable, high-density wireless infrastructure.

27. The university selects a **controller**-based architecture where all APs are managed by a central wireless LAN controller rather than configuring each AP individually. ✓

28. For lecture halls with 300 students, the design calls for one AP per **50** users to ensure adequate bandwidth and airtime fairness. ✓

29. In the 2.4 GHz band, the channel plan assigns only channels 1, **6**, and 11 to avoid co-channel interference between adjacent APs. ✓

30. Each AP requires **PoE** (Power over Ethernet) from the network switch, eliminating the need for separate power outlets at each mounting location. ✓

31. Before deployment, the team conducts a **site** survey using specialized software to create heat maps and validate AP placement. ✓

## Part 7: Scenario - Wireless Roaming Design

The university's network team must ensure seamless wireless connectivity as students move between buildings and floors.

32. The team enables 802.11r (Fast **BSS** Transition) to reduce roaming handoff time by pre-authenticating with target APs. ✓

33. 802.11k provides Radio **Resource** Management, allowing APs to send neighbor reports so clients know which APs are available for roaming. ✓

34. 802.11v enables BSS **Transition** Management, allowing the network to steer clients to less congested APs. ✓

35. To speed up roaming for WPA2-Enterprise, the network caches the PMK using **Opportunistic** Key Caching (OKC) so clients skip full re-authentication. ✓

36. The roaming **threshold** is configured to determine the minimum signal strength at which a client should begin looking for a better AP. ✓
hints: Site surveys come in three flavors: one where you walk around and connect, one where you walk around and just listen, and one done entirely with software models.
In the 2.4 GHz band, channels 1, 6, and 11 are the magic non-overlapping trio — the middle one is channel 6.
Each 2.4 GHz channel is actually 22 MHz wide, which is why only 3 out of 11 channels don't overlap.
DFS is a regulatory requirement that protects radar systems — APs must detect and avoid radar by changing frequency.
TPC controls how much power an AP radiates — the P stands for Power and the C for Control.
Wi-Fi 6E added a whole new band beyond 5 GHz — think of the next whole number after 5.
For optimal coverage, APs should be mounted high — ceilings are the standard recommendation, typically 10-15 feet up.
Omnidirectional antennas radiate like a donut pattern; directional antennas focus energy like a flashlight beam.
Signal strength in dBm is always negative for received signals — a value of -65 dBm is considered good for most indoor applications.
Heat maps use intuitive color coding — think traffic lights where green means go (good signal).

testCases.description: Should identify radar detection in DFS
Should identify PoE for AP power
